summary: Gruber responds to Dan Frommer's criticism of the Apple TV 3.0 software update, arguing that the complaints are largely misguided. He defends Apple's positioning of Apple TV as a dedicated iTunes Store front-end rather than an open platform, noting that those who want openness should use a Mac Mini. He dismisses the Blu-ray suggestion as counter to Apple's strategic bet on digital distribution, and points out that the app store and gaming suggestions would require new hardware that doesn't yet exist. His real complaint is the limited movie selection available through iTunes.
key_points: Apple TV is intentionally a closed, curated iTunes Store front-end — not an open platform — and Apple has no interest in changing that
Criticizing a software update for not including features that would require new hardware is unfair, though Apple does need to update the hardware eventually
Apple has made a deliberate strategic decision to ignore Blu-ray entirely, betting on iTunes Store for HD content
The real weakness of Apple TV is not hardware or software but the limited catalog of rentable movies on iTunes
money_quotes: Apple TV is not a Mac, it's a top-to-bottom Apple experience device.
If you want access to everything, use a Mac Mini — more powerful, open to just about anything, but also a lot more fiddly.
Apple seems to have made a decision to ignore Blu-ray across the board, at least for now. Apple's answer for HD movies is the iTunes Store.

You can argue that Apple should have released major new Apple TV hardware in time for this holiday season, but it's not fair to complain that a software update doesn't include features that would require new hardware.
My biggest Apple TV complaint has nothing to do with its hardware or software, but instead remains the paltry number of movies available through iTunes — especially the number of rentable movies.
key_insight: Apple TV's real limitation isn't its closed hardware or software philosophy but the thin content catalog backing it, and criticizing a software update for lacking features that require nonexistent hardware misses the point.
